title: The Lip Reader

jokes.text: Professional tennis... I don't understand the shushing. Why are they always shushing? Don't the players know that we're there?
jokes.setup: Tennis crowds are known for being quiet and shushing people
jokes.punchline: Absurd suggestion that players might not know spectators exist
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry observational humor questioning social conventions with absurd logic

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I think? I think the players don't even know the crowd is there. I think they could be playing in front of thousands of people or nobody, they wouldn't know the difference. That's why everyone has to be so quiet — the players might think they're alone!

jokes.text: Should we duck behind the seats so they don't see us watching them?
jokes.setup: Following up on the idea that players don't know spectators are there
jokes.punchline: Extending the absurd logic to its ridiculous conclusion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that builds on the previous joke's premise

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So by that logic, if no one's watching, the game doesn't exist. The players are just out there playing for nobody.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what if everyone stops going to games? Do the teams just disappear?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Poof! Gone. They're erased from existence.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's why you gotta go to the games, Jerry. You're keeping them alive!

jokes.text: To me, tennis is basically just Ping-Pong... and the players are standing on the table. That's all it is.
jokes.setup: Comparing tennis to another racket sport
jokes.punchline: Reducing tennis to an absurd visual metaphor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reductive comparison with vivid, absurd imagery that's very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Tennis is just a game where two people stand on opposite ends of a court and hit a ball back and forth. You know what that is? That's not a sport, that's a couple having a really long argument.

jokes.text: And that goofy scoring. You win one point, all of a sudden you're up by 15. Two points, 30-love. 30-love.
jokes.setup: Tennis scoring is indeed confusing to outsiders
jokes.punchline: The repetition of '30-love' sets up the next joke
jokes.explanation: Standard observational setup that works primarily to set up the punchline that follows

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what's confusing about tennis scoring? They say '30-love.' Love? Who came up with that? In what sport do you want your opponent to love you? 'Yeah, I'm down 0-30, but at least he loves me.'

jokes.text: Sounds like an English call girl. 'That'll be 30, love. And could you be a little quieter next time, please?'
jokes.setup: The tennis score '30-love' sounds like something else
jokes.punchline: Transforms tennis terminology into prostitution joke with British accent
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay that transforms innocent tennis term into risqué double entendre with perfect delivery

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I love about tennis? The terminology. '30-love.' That sounds like something you'd hear in a brothel in London.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: 'Thirty love, guv'nor. That'll be fifty quid.'

jokes.text: Are these seats unbelievable, or what?
jokes.setup: George is excited about their seats
jokes.punchline: Classic George enthusiasm about something mundane
jokes.explanation: Basic character moment that establishes George's personality but not particularly funny

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: These are great seats! These are really great seats! You know what? I'm gonna remember these seats for the rest of my life. When I'm on my deathbed, I'm gonna think about these seats.

jokes.text: Twenty-five? You don't have anything higher? What, are you on Mercury? I need higher.
jokes.setup: George wants higher SPF sunblock
jokes.punchline: Mercury reference for extreme sun protection needs
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing George's neurotic attention to detail with space reference

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I need a higher SPF sunblock. I'm very fair-skinned.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: How high?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: SPF 100.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: SPF 100? George, they don't make SPF 100.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, they should. I'm telling you, I need it. One time I was out in the sun for twenty minutes, I looked like Mercury.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Mercury?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The planet. Gray.

jokes.text: This has PABA in it. I need PABA-free. You got a problem with PABA? I have a problem with PABA.
jokes.setup: George is particular about sunblock ingredients
jokes.punchline: The escalating PABA obsession
jokes.explanation: Classic neurotic George behavior with good repetition and escalation

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: PABA? What is PABA? I need to know what PABA is.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's just an ingredient in sunscreen, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Just an ingredient? JUST an ingredient? Do you know what PABA does to the skin? I read about PABA. PABA is poison.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's not poison.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You don't know that. Nobody knows that. They're putting PABA on people and we don't even know the long-term effects. In fifty years, everyone's going to have PABA poisoning.

jokes.text: You don't even know what PABA is. I know enough to stay away from it.
jokes.setup: Elaine challenges George's PABA knowledge
jokes.punchline: George admits ignorance while maintaining his position
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - maintaining strong opinions based on no knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What do you know about PABA?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: PABA? Para-aminobenzoic acid. It's a B vitamin. It's in sunscreen.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's wrong.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, I'm pretty sure that's right.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's not a B vitamin.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, whether it's a B vitamin or not, it's definitely in sunscreen.

jokes.text: You're bringing a date to a party? No good? A party's a bad date situation.
jokes.setup: George mentions bringing Gwen to a party
jokes.punchline: Jerry's theory about parties being bad for dates
jokes.explanation: Good observational premise that many can relate to

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm bringing Gwen to a party.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't bring a date to a party. Parties are where you go when you can't get a date. It's like bringing a chess set to a strip club.

jokes.text: You could be with J. Edgar Hoover. You don't wanna sit and talk with Hoover all night. You want to circulate.
jokes.setup: Continuing the party date theory
jokes.punchline: Random choice of J. Edgar Hoover as example
jokes.explanation: Odd historical figure choice makes the example more memorable and funny

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, there's a whole system to this dating thing. It's all about the party date theory.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You take a girl to a party, you introduce her around, suddenly you're a guy with a girlfriend.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like J. Edgar Hoover. Nobody knew who he was until he showed up at a party with a dress on.

jokes.text: Why'd you pick Hoover? Is he that interesting to talk to? Well, with the law enforcement and the cross-dressing... seems like an interesting guy.
jokes.setup: George questions Jerry's Hoover reference
jokes.punchline: Jerry justifies it with Hoover's rumored cross-dressing
jokes.explanation: Smart callback that explains the random choice with historical scandal

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Hoover?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, you know, J. Edgar Hoover. He was a cross-dresser.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So? So he was the head of the FBI in a dress!

jokes.text: That woman is absolutely stunning. Who, the Croat? No, not the Croat. The lineswoman.
jokes.setup: Jerry points out a beautiful woman
jokes.punchline: George's assumption about which woman is wrong
jokes.explanation: Simple misdirection joke that sets up the lineswoman plot

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look at her.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Where?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Right there, the blonde in the blue dress.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, she's beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not her.

jokes.text: That is the most beautiful lineswoman I've ever seen. Yeah, she's a BL. BL? Beautiful lineswoman.
jokes.setup: Jerry admires the lineswoman
jokes.punchline: George creates acronym 'BL' for 'beautiful lineswoman'
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld neologism creation with typical character specificity

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what we should call her? BL. Beautiful Lineswoman.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: BL? That's what you're going with?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? It works!

jokes.text: Jerry? Jerry? [No response - Jerry is transfixed by the lineswoman]
jokes.setup: George tries to get Jerry's attention about going to concession stand
jokes.punchline: Jerry is so mesmerized he doesn't respond at all
jokes.explanation: Visual gag showing Jerry's infatuation through his complete non-response

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry, Jerry! Hey, you want to go to the concession stand?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Hello?

jokes.text: Why does everything have to have a social component?
jokes.setup: Elaine complaining about chatty cab drivers
jokes.punchline: Reducing human interaction to an unwanted 'social component'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Elaine misanthropy distilled into clinical language

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I don't want to talk to cab drivers. I just want to sit there quietly and go to my destination. But they always want to chat. It's like they think we're friends or something. I don't need that social component. I'm paying you to drive, not to be my therapist.

jokes.text: See, the stagecoach. Now, that would've been a good situation for me. I'm in the coach, and the driver's way up there on the stage.
jokes.setup: Elaine wants to avoid talking to drivers
jokes.punchline: Romanticizing historical transportation for antisocial reasons
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd historical fantasy based on antisocial motivations - very Elaine

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know what I've been thinking? Horses. We should all just go back to riding horses everywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Horses? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Because then you wouldn't have to talk to anyone! You'd just be on your horse, alone, going where you need to go. No drivers, no conversation, no eye contact.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you want to go back to the 1800s just to avoid small talk?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm not saying it's crazy, but I'm not NOT saying it either.

jokes.text: Hey! There's George. Holy cow. It's a scorcher.
jokes.setup: TV announcers notice George on camera
jokes.punchline: George is clearly sweating profusely or looking uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Sets up the ice cream visual gag that becomes important later

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: We're getting reports that George Costanza is sweating through his shirt.

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: He appears to be very uncomfortable on camera.

jokes.text: Boy, I bet you that guy could cover a lot of court. Hey, buddy, they got a new invention. It's called a napkin.
jokes.setup: Announcer commenting on George eating messily on camera
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic suggestion about using napkins
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy that will have consequences for George's relationship

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: Well, here we see George Costanza, eating like a man who's never seen food before. Perhaps he's heard of napkins?

jokes.text: Yeah, a lot of people read to relax. Not me. You know what I do?
jokes.setup: Driver trying to make conversation about reading
jokes.punchline: Setting up his weird relaxation method
jokes.explanation: Standard setup for the jumble punchline

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: You read books?

dialogue.speaker: Passenger
dialogue.line: Yeah, I read.

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: I don't read. I tried reading once. Couldn't do it. My mind kept wandering. So now when I want to relax, I just sit in the car, turn off the engine, and think about how I'm not reading.

jokes.text: The jumble. Hey, do you make a book of jumbles?
jokes.setup: Driver's unusual relaxation method
jokes.punchline: Word puzzle games as stress relief, then trying to pitch it
jokes.explanation: Mildly absurd character detail that feels authentic to annoying cab driver

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: You know what I do to relax? Word puzzles. Crosswords, anagrams, you name it. Keeps the mind sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: So I'm thinking, why not make a game out of it? Something people would pay for.

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: I call it 'Wordmaster.' You get letters, you make words, whoever makes the most words wins.

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: It's genius! It's relaxing AND competitive. What do you think?

jokes.text: I'm gonna have to be honest with you. I'm going deaf. Going deaf? What?
jokes.setup: Elaine pretends to be going deaf to avoid conversation
jokes.punchline: Driver's immediate 'What?' response
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup for the mishearing that follows

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I can't hear you. I've gone deaf.

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: Hey, what about a hearing aid? Am I fearing AIDS? Oh, yeah, sure. Who isn't? But, you know, you gotta live your life.
jokes.setup: Driver asks about hearing aid
jokes.punchline: Elaine mishears as 'fearing AIDS' and gives inappropriate response
jokes.explanation: Excellent mishearing joke that creates inappropriate but funny response to serious topic

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: Do you have a hearing aid?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Fearing AIDS? No, I'm very careful.

jokes.text: I'll psych myself into it like those people that walk across the hot coals. But they're not mocked and humiliated when they get to the other side.
jokes.setup: Jerry comparing approaching women to fire walking
jokes.punchline: George points out the key difference in outcomes
jokes.explanation: Smart comparison that highlights the social risks of approaching women vs. physical challenges

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Approaching a woman is like fire walking. You have to commit fully or you'll get burned.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: There's one key difference though.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: With fire walking, some people actually make it across.

jokes.text: If you go through that wall and become one of those guys... I'll be left here on this side. Take me with you. I can't.
jokes.setup: George fears Jerry becoming confident with women
jokes.punchline: Treating confidence as a place you can physically travel to
jokes.explanation: Perfect George neurosis about being left behind, treating confidence as geographical

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, once you get confident with women, you're gonna leave me behind. You're gonna move to Confidence City and I'm gonna be stuck here in Insecurity Town.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, Confidence City isn't a real place.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, it should be! And there's probably a bridge between the two towns, and you're gonna burn it behind you!

jokes.text: What are you gonna say? I don't know. 'Hi.' You think you're going to the other side with 'hi'? You're not gonna make it.
jokes.setup: Jerry's minimal approach strategy
jokes.punchline: George's pessimistic assessment using the 'other side' metaphor
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the confidence metaphor with typical George pessimism

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, if you tried the opposite, if every instinct you have is wrong, then the opposite would have to be right.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm out there Jerry and I'm lovin' some of these broads!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Are you?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, they're not pretty, they're not smart, but their all sweet. What's not to love?

jokes.text: 749 Bleeker. The party's Hanks. Tom Hanks. Tom Hanks? After me, you're picking up Tom Hanks? I love him.
jokes.setup: Dispatcher mentions a pickup
jokes.punchline: It's actually Tom Hanks, and Elaine's excitement
jokes.explanation: Celebrity name drop that sets up Elaine's hearing revelation

dialogue.speaker: Dispatcher
dialogue.line: We have a pickup for you.

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: Where?

dialogue.speaker: Dispatcher
dialogue.line: Upper West Side.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait, who is it?

dialogue.speaker: Dispatcher
dialogue.line: Tom Hanks.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Tom Hanks?! Oh my God!

jokes.text: So I guess your hearing goes in and out, huh?
jokes.setup: Driver realizes Elaine can hear selectively
jokes.punchline: Calling out her obvious deception
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Elaine getting caught in her lie

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: You can't hear me, right? You can't hear me!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: You heard that! You heard that! You're faking it!

jokes.text: I think you made that whole thing up. Yeah, yeah. I know your type. You're too good to make conversation with someone like me.
jokes.setup: Driver realizes Elaine was lying about being deaf
jokes.punchline: His indignant response about her snobbery
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment as Elaine's deception backfires

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: You're not deaf! You were just ignoring me because you thought you were too good for me!

jokes.text: And Mr. Tom Hanks... May I say he, too, would be disgusted by your behavior.
jokes.setup: Driver is angry at Elaine's deception
jokes.punchline: Invoking Tom Hanks as moral authority
jokes.explanation: Absurdly escalated moral indignation using celebrity as character witness

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: You lied to me! You said you were going to the airport!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I know, I'm sorry, I just—

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: Tom Hanks would never do this. Tom Hanks is honest. Tom Hanks is a good man.

jokes.text: Oh, like that's some kind of a cool job. I know your type. Think you're too good for everyone.
jokes.setup: Jerry is trying to approach the lineswoman
jokes.punchline: He's inadvertently insulting her job while trying to get her attention
jokes.explanation: Jerry's failed attempt to get attention becomes an insult instead

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Excuse me, miss? You know, you'd be really attractive if you weren't doing this job.

jokes.text: Oh, well... What, are you deaf? Bingo.
jokes.setup: Jerry sarcastically asks if she's deaf when she doesn't respond
jokes.punchline: His realization that she actually is deaf
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - sarcastic question becomes literal truth with great economy

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What, are you deaf?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh my God, she's deaf.

jokes.text: When I was 8, I had a deaf cousin who lived with us for about a year. Of course, you know, I haven't been able to do it in a while.
jokes.setup: Kramer claims to know sign language
jokes.punchline: Admitting he's rusty at something he learned as a child
jokes.explanation: Typical Kramer random skill with qualification that makes it less useful

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know sign language. I learned it as a kid.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know sign language?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm a little rusty though.

jokes.text: When I shoo squirrels away, I always say, 'Get out of here.' I never, ever throw things at them and try to injure them... like other people.
jokes.setup: Elaine defending her moral character
jokes.punchline: Weirdly specific example about squirrel treatment
jokes.explanation: Absurdly specific moral position that reveals Elaine's neurotic self-image

jokes.text: And when I see freaks in the street, I never, ever stare at them. And yet I'm careful not to look away. See, because I wanna make the freaks feel comfortable.
jokes.setup: Elaine continuing her moral defense
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate use of 'freaks' while claiming sensitivity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Elaine - trying to be sensitive while using insensitive language

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm very sensitive to people's needs and feelings. I would never intentionally hurt anyone. But sometimes people are just freaks, you know? I mean, we're all freaks in our own way.

jokes.text: That's nice for the freaks.
jokes.setup: Elaine's explanation about being considerate to 'freaks'
jokes.punchline: Kramer's matter-of-fact response using the same inappropriate term
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan callback that extends the inappropriate language naturally

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You have to be considerate to freaks.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, yeah, I mean, they're people too.

jokes.text: I think perhaps you've overlooked one of the key aspects of this activity. It's ball boys, not ball men. There are no ball men.
jokes.setup: Kramer wants to be a ball boy
jokes.punchline: Jerry points out the age requirement inherent in the name
jokes.explanation: Good observational logic delivered with perfect Jerry deadpan

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna be a ball boy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't be a ball boy. You have to be a boy.

jokes.text: I think he's right. I've never seen a ball man. Well, there ought to be ball men.
jokes.setup: George agrees about the lack of ball men
jokes.punchline: Kramer's indignant response about age discrimination
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Kramer's determination to break barriers

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Age discrimination! That's what this is!

jokes.text: You wanna be a ball man, go ahead. Break the ball barrier.
jokes.setup: Kramer's determination to be a ball man
jokes.punchline: Jerry's encouragement with pun on 'color barrier'
jokes.explanation: Clever pun that elevates the trivial to historical significance

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, Kramer, I think you should go for it. You've got the passion, you've got the dedication. Don't let anyone tell you that you can't be a ball man.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, I mean, you'd be breaking the color barrier.

jokes.text: He was at the snack bar eating a hot fudge sundae. He had it all over his face. He's wearing that chocolate on his face like a beard.
jokes.setup: Elaine describing George's TV appearance
jokes.punchline: The vivid description of messy eating
jokes.explanation: Good visual description that makes George's embarrassment vivid

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You should see him on TV. He's like a caveman. He's shoveling food into his mouth like he's never eaten before. There's pieces flying everywhere. He's got sauce all over his face, his shirt, his hands. It's like watching someone eat in fast-forward. And the worst part? He keeps talking with his mouth full, so food is just spraying out. It's absolutely disgusting.

jokes.text: You're giving me the 'it's not you, it's me' routine? I invented 'it's not you, it's me.' Nobody tells me it's them, not me.
jokes.setup: Gwen tries to break up with George using the standard line
jokes.punchline: George's outrage at having his own breakup technique used on him
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - George's ego and ownership of manipulation tactics. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Gwen
dialogue.line: It's not you, it's me.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, no, no, no. I'm using that line! I've used that line more than anyone in the history of dating. Nobody uses that line better than me. I've perfected it. It's the only line that works. You can't use my line!

jokes.text: All right. George, it's you. You are damn right it's me.
jokes.setup: George forces Gwen to be direct
jokes.punchline: George's pride in being the problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect George - turning being dumped into a point of pride and ownership

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what? I don't want you to hold back. I want you to tell me exactly what you think of me. Be direct.

dialogue.speaker: Gwen
dialogue.line: You're the problem.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Finally! Someone who tells it like it is!

jokes.text: Well, apparently word's out.
jokes.setup: George's breakup technique being used against him
jokes.punchline: Treating the phrase like a trade secret that's been leaked
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry deadpan that treats common phrase like insider information

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She used my line on me! 'It's not you, it's me.' She said it first!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what's the problem?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'what's the problem?' That's my line! I've been perfecting that line for years!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, it's not a patent.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It should be! I'm going to call my lawyer. This is intellectual property theft!

jokes.text: And the announcers, they made a couple of cracks about you. That you had ice cream all over your face. They were talking about how funny you looked.
jokes.setup: George was on TV eating messily
jokes.punchline: The announcers were openly mocking him
jokes.explanation: Escalates George's embarrassment but fairly standard cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I saw that. They were like, 'Look at this guy, he's eating like an animal!'

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: They were mocking me! On television!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, what did you expect? You had food all over your face.

jokes.text: Well, I'll tell you, it wasn't a pretty sight.
jokes.setup: George's messy eating on TV
jokes.punchline: Kramer's blunt assessment
jokes.explanation: Standard Kramer bluntness but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Look at you, you're like a barbarian!

jokes.text: She sees you with hot fudge on your face and ends it? You think she'd be that superficial? Why not? I would be.
jokes.setup: Jerry questioning if Gwen would break up over the TV incident
jokes.punchline: George's immediate honest admission of his own superficiality
jokes.explanation: Great George self-awareness - admitting his own shallowness without shame

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You really think she'd break up with you over that?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? I'd break up with me over that.

jokes.text: One-1000. Two-1000. Three-1000... I heard you really inhaled that thing.
jokes.setup: Kramer timing how long George was on camera
jokes.punchline: The methodical counting and eating description
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy timing with Kramer's precision

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: He was on for like, what, two minutes?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Two minutes? That's a long time to be on camera.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I was counting. One Mississippi, two Mississippi, three Mississippi...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You were counting Mississippis?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, and eating a sandwich. One Mississippi, *takes bite*, two Mississippi, *takes bite*...

jokes.text: If a couple breaks up with plans to go to a neutral place, who withdraws? What's the etiquette?
jokes.setup: George and Gwen both planned to attend the same party
jokes.punchline: Treating post-breakup social situations like formal etiquette
jokes.explanation: Great observational premise that many people can relate to - very Seinfeld

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So we're both going to the party. What do we do?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You just act normal. You saw each other, you said hello.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Normal? Jerry, there's nothing normal about this. We need rules. Structure. This is like the UN. We need a buffer zone.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A buffer zone?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: At least fifteen feet at all times. And we don't make eye contact. If we're in the same room, I'm looking at the left wall, she's looking at the right wall.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you're not negotiating a peace treaty.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Aren't I? Because one wrong move, one accidental brush of the shoulders, and this whole thing blows up. We need something in writing. A signed agreement.

jokes.text: She's the breaker. He's the breakee. He needs to get on with his life.
jokes.setup: The etiquette question about who should withdraw
jokes.punchline: Creating breakup terminology with 'breaker' and 'breakee'
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld neologism creation that's both logical and funny

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So there's etiquette about who should withdraw from the relationship first?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Of course! The person who initiates the breakup is the 'breaker' and the person being broken up with is the 'breakee.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The 'breakee'? That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? It's a perfectly good term! You got your breaker and your breakee.

jokes.text: He's the loser. She's the victor. To the victor belong the spoils.
jokes.setup: The breakup etiquette debate
jokes.punchline: Applying historical military saying to dating
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation using classical reference to make breakups sound like warfare

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You can't just break up with someone over the phone. That's brutal.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? It's faster.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's cowardly. You have to do it in person.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what, I'm supposed to fly across the country to break up with someone face to face?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's the decent thing to do.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I once broke up with a girl by leaving a note on her answering machine.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's despicable!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I was in a hurry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what the problem is? There's no rules anymore. It's like war - all is fair in love and dating.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Retreat is not an option.

jokes.text: 'To the victor go the spoils.'
jokes.setup: George repeating Elaine's phrase
jokes.punchline: His bitter repetition showing he's dwelling on it
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing George's tendency to ruminate

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: These pretzels are making me thirsty.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: These pretzels are making me thirsty.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: These pretzels are making me thirsty.

jokes.text: I was just gonna wander the streets.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks what George is doing tonight
jokes.punchline: George's pathetic evening plans
jokes.explanation: Perfect George pathos - making loneliness sound almost romantic

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what are you doing tonight?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm going home, I'm going to sit on my couch, eat some cookies, watch some TV, maybe call a woman if I get the urge.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What do you want from me? I'm a simple man with simple needs.

jokes.text: So I've got ice cream all over my face. There were no napkins there. Whoever is responsible for stocking that concession stand... cost me a relationship.
jokes.setup: George explaining his breakup theory
jokes.punchline: Blaming concession stand logistics for relationship failure
jokes.explanation: Classic George - elaborate theory to avoid personal responsibility

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You see, the problem with us was the concession stand. She wanted popcorn, I wanted candy. We couldn't agree on anything. It's a relationship killer.

jokes.text: Is this considered signing? Do you do this when you ask for the check?
jokes.setup: George trying to communicate with Laura
jokes.punchline: Assuming any gesture is sign language
jokes.explanation: Standard fish-out-of-water humor with George's cultural assumptions

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you doing? Are you trying to tell me something?

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: No, I'm just...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because I'm telling you, if you're going to speak to me in sign language, you're going to have to slow down. I have no idea what you're saying.

jokes.text: 'It's not you. It's me.'
jokes.setup: Laura reading the lips of a couple breaking up
jokes.punchline: The same line George invented being used by others
jokes.explanation: Great callback showing the universality of George's 'invention'

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: He's saying... 'It's not you, it's me.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's the line! That's the line George came up with!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's using George's line?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's everywhere now. Everyone's using it.

jokes.text: We bring her to the party... and she tells me what Gwen is saying about me.
jokes.setup: George realizes Laura's lip reading ability could be useful
jokes.punchline: His immediate plan to exploit her skill for personal gain
jokes.explanation: Perfect George scheming - immediately seeing how to use someone's disability selfishly

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. You can read lips?

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Do you know what this means? We could go to restaurants, sit at other tables, find out what people are ordering before we order. We'd always know what's good!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Or we could go to a movie, sit behind the studio executives, find out what they're working on before anyone else knows!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is gold, Jerry! Gold!

jokes.text: She's not a novelty act, George... that you hire out for weddings and bar mitzvahs.
jokes.setup: George wants to use Laura's lip reading
jokes.punchline: Jerry's comparison to entertainment performers
jokes.explanation: Good Jerry moral stance with specific cultural references

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I could use Laura to lip read. I could find out what people are saying about me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's like having a secret spy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You'd be like Carnac the Magnificent, except instead of predicting the future, you'd be finding out what people think of you behind your back.

jokes.text: Look, it's a skill, just like juggling. She probably enjoys showing it off.
jokes.setup: Jerry's objection to using Laura
jokes.punchline: George's elaborate rationalization comparing disability to circus skill
jokes.explanation: Classic George rationalization - making exploitation sound like a favor

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What's the big deal? So she's got one arm. You know, I once knew a guy who could tie his shoes with his teeth. Nobody made a fuss about that. In fact, we thought it was pretty cool. So this woman, she's got one arm - that's her thing. That's her talent. We all got something we're good at. I'm good at taking naps. You're good at complaining. She's good at... doing everything with one arm. It's not a disability, it's a skill!

jokes.text: Laura, George was wondering if... Sure. I'll do it.
jokes.setup: Jerry reluctantly asking Laura to help George
jokes.punchline: She agrees before he even finishes asking
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Jerry's hesitation vs Laura's enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look, I know this is a lot to ask, but George really needs—

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You didn't even let me finish.

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: I know what you're going to say, and the answer is yes.

jokes.text: You got a problem with 6?
jokes.setup: Jerry says he'll pick Laura up at 6
jokes.punchline: Laura's confused reaction sets up the mishearing
jokes.explanation: Setup for the six/sex confusion that comes later

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'll pick you up at 6.

jokes.text: Hey, pops, isn't there a better way to spend your twilight years? Well, I may be old, but I'm spry.
jokes.setup: Coordinator questioning Kramer's age for ball boy tryouts
jokes.punchline: Kramer's defiant response about being spry
jokes.explanation: Good age-appropriate humor with Kramer's characteristic confidence

dialogue.speaker: Ball boy coordinator
dialogue.line: You're a little old for this, aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm spry! I'm very spry!

jokes.text: See, I was saying 'six,' but she thought I was saying 'sex.' We straightened the whole thing out.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining the confusion with Laura
jokes.punchline: The innocent mishearing that sounds sexual
jokes.explanation: Standard mishearing joke that sets up the lip reading competence question

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She thought I said something completely different. I said, 'I'd like to get your number,' and she heard, 'I'd like to get you numb-er.' Like I was trying to make her more numb!

jokes.text: 'Six' and 'sex' are close. It's two completely different sounds. And:
jokes.setup: George defending the confusion
jokes.punchline: Jerry's exaggerated demonstration of the different mouth positions
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy potential with Jerry demonstrating lip positions

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? There's a big difference!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, there's a big difference? Let me show you the difference.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: (demonstrates with exaggerated mouth positions) See? This is a smile. This is a smirk. This is a sneer. This is a grimace.

jokes.text: They said they never saw anybody go after balls with such gusto.
jokes.setup: Kramer explaining his ball boy success
jokes.punchline: The unintentionally suggestive language about 'going after balls'
jokes.explanation: Mild double entendre that works because of Kramer's innocence

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I was great at it. I mean, I was going after balls all day long. I was diving for balls, I was stretching for balls. My leg was up here, my arm was over there.

jokes.text: If we could just harness this power and use it for our own personal gain... there'd be no stopping us.
jokes.setup: Jerry comparing lip reading to superpowers
jokes.punchline: Treating it like a world domination tool
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that treats mundane skill like supervillain capability

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what lip reading is? It's a superpower. I mean, think about it. You're sitting across from someone, you can't hear them, but you know exactly what they're saying. That's incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But here's the thing — nobody uses it for good. Nobody's like, 'I'm going to use my lip reading powers to help people.' No, the first thing you think is world domination. You're sitting in a restaurant, you see two people talking, you're reading their lips, and you're thinking, 'I could take over the world with this information.'

jokes.text: Let me use her once. Can't do it, Newman.
jokes.setup: Newman wants to borrow the lip reader
jokes.punchline: Jerry's immediate refusal to his nemesis
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamics showing Jerry/Newman antagonism

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Jerry, I need to borrow your lip reader.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: When you control the mail, you control information.
jokes.setup: Newman's frustrated threat to Jerry
jokes.punchline: Grandiose statement about postal worker power
jokes.explanation: Excellent Newman line - paranoid delusions of postal power with perfect delivery

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: You think you're better than me? I'm a postal worker! I could have your mail rerouted to Siberia. I could lose your social security check. I could send your magazine subscriptions to any address I want!

jokes.text: Oh, sorry. You'll have to forgive me. I can't hear a damn thing. Went to a rock concert last night at the Garden.
jokes.setup: Same driver who caught Elaine faking deafness
jokes.punchline: Now he's actually having hearing problems
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - the driver who exposed fake deafness now has hearing problems

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: Hey, you! Yeah, you! Get in!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh no, not you again.

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: What? WHAT?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I said, not you again!

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: Huh? I can't hear you! Speak up!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You can't hear me?

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Never mind.

jokes.text: It's a heavy metal group, Metalli-something. Ca. What? Ca.
jokes.setup: Driver can't remember the band name
jokes.punchline: Elaine tries to help complete 'Metallica' but he can't hear
jokes.explanation: Good generational gap humor with the hearing impairment adding irony

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: What's the name of that band? You know, the heavy metal band?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Metallica?

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Metallica!

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: I can't hear you!

jokes.text: Some woman's idea of a joke.
jokes.setup: Driver explaining his hearing loss
jokes.punchline: Blaming a woman for the concert tickets (referring to Elaine)
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to Elaine's attempt to make amends backfiring

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: I lost my hearing at a concert. My girlfriend at the time, she bought the tickets.

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: It's all her fault.

jokes.text: Hey, this is my friend you helped become the first ball man. Congratulations.
jokes.setup: Jerry introducing Kramer's achievement
jokes.punchline: Laura congratulating Kramer but can't hear her response
jokes.explanation: Setup for Laura's response about Kramer

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Laura, you're not going to believe what Kramer just did.

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: What? That's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, right?

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: What did he say?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have no idea. I can't hear a word he's saying.

jokes.text: She doesn't know what she's talking about.
jokes.setup: Laura apparently said something negative about Kramer
jokes.punchline: Kramer's defensive dismissal
jokes.explanation: Standard defensive reaction, sets up anticipation for what Laura said

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, maybe I don't want to hear what Laura has to say about me!

jokes.text: But you should try and find out everything you possibly can. In fact, I'll even stay all the way over on the other side of the room... just so there's no chance of me overhearing anything.
jokes.setup: George claiming he doesn't want to know what Gwen says
jokes.punchline: Elaborate setup to ensure he can spy without being caught
jokes.explanation: Perfect George manipulation - elaborate reverse psychology to get what he wants

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't want to know what she says. I don't want to hear it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what are you gonna do?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm gonna position myself behind the mail boxes in the alcove by the front desk where I can see directly into her apartment.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're gonna hide behind the mail boxes?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'll just be checking my mail. If anyone asks, I'm checking my mail.

jokes.text: 'Hi, Gwen.' 'Hi, Tide.' High tide? 'Hi, Todd.'
jokes.setup: Laura beginning to lip read
jokes.punchline: Mishearing 'Todd' as 'Tide'
jokes.explanation: Good setup showing lip reading isn't perfect, foreshadowing the big mistake

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: Wait, did he say 'Tide'?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, Todd. His name is Todd.

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought he said the detergent.

jokes.text: 'That's interesting. I love carrots, but I hate carrot soup. And I hate peas, but I love pea soup.'
jokes.setup: Laura reading lips of random conversation
jokes.punchline: Mundane food preferences being treated as spy intelligence
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between the spy setup and mundane reality of most conversations

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: I'm reading their lips. He said 'I like tuna on toast.' She said 'I prefer mayo.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: This is classified information. These people's sandwich preferences could change everything.

jokes.text: 'Why don't you stick around, and we can sleep together.' What? 'You want me to sleep with you?'
jokes.setup: Laura misreading lips about cleaning up
jokes.punchline: Major mishearing that changes innocent suggestion to sexual proposition
jokes.explanation: Major plot-driving mishearing that creates the central conflict

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: You should really clean up your apartment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What did you say?

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: I said you should clean up your apartment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, I heard 'come up and see me sometime.'

jokes.text: 'I don't wanna sleep alone.' And she says... Oh, boy. 'Love to.'
jokes.setup: Continuing the mishearing
jokes.punchline: Laura's 'Oh, boy' reaction and the apparent acceptance
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with Laura's shocked reaction adding to the misunderstanding

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: Oh, boy.

jokes.text: All right, that's it. You get rid of me, now the two of you are gonna sleep together?
jokes.setup: George believes the misheard conversation
jokes.punchline: His outraged confrontation based on false information
jokes.explanation: Perfect George jealousy and possessiveness based on complete misunderstanding

jokes.text: No, I didn't. I said 'sweep.' Let's sweep together, like with a broom. Cleaning up.
jokes.setup: Todd trying to clarify the misunderstanding
jokes.punchline: The innocent explanation that should resolve everything
jokes.explanation: Standard clarification that shows how innocent the original conversation was

jokes.text: Cut it. Would you stop signing?! They said 'sweep together,' idiots. Not 'sleep together.'
jokes.setup: The confusion continuing to escalate
jokes.punchline: Gwen calling them idiots and revealing the truth
jokes.explanation: Good resolution showing everyone how stupid the misunderstanding was

dialogue.speaker: Gwen
dialogue.line: You idiots! I'm not even a real person. I'm George's imaginary girlfriend!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? No, you're real!

dialogue.speaker: Gwen
dialogue.line: I exist only in your mind, George. Every conversation we've had, every moment together - it was all in your head.

jokes.text: Look, I know how to sign. My eye! My eye!
jokes.setup: Kramer trying to demonstrate sign language knowledge
jokes.punchline: Accidentally injuring himself or someone else
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer physical comedy - his confidence leading to disaster

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I know sign language. Watch this.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: *makes exaggerated signing gestures, accidentally hits himself in the face*

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Ow! I think I just signed 'ouch.'

jokes.text: Thus ends the great ball man experiment.
jokes.setup: Kramer's ball man career presumably ending in disaster
jokes.punchline: Announcer's dry commentary on the failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan callback that treats Kramer's failure with mock gravity

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: And that's the end of Kramer's career as a ball man.

jokes.text: Hey, how about that ball man injuring Monica Seles? Wasn't that something? I'm deaf.
jokes.setup: Reporter asking Laura about the tennis incident
jokes.punchline: Laura's simple response that she can't hear the question
jokes.explanation: Good ironic ending showing Laura's straightforward honesty about her disability

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: Can you tell us about the tennis incident?

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I can't hear you.

jokes.text: Unless the waiter's not too sharp. Then you gotta total it up. 'I want the check.' They come over, 'Do you want the check?' 'No, I want to be pen pals. Can't you see what I'm doing here? I'm trying to be cool and impress people.'
jokes.setup: Jerry's closing monologue about the check gesture
jokes.punchline: Escalating frustration with obtuse waiters and admission of trying to look cool
jokes.explanation: Good closing bit that ties back to earlier visual communication theme with Jerry's typical self-awareness

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I think the check gesture is one of the most important gestures in the world. It's this little scribbling motion in the air that says 'I'm done, bring me the bill.' But waiters? They don't get it. You do the gesture, they look at you like you just invented a new dance move.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you do it again, bigger. Still nothing. Now you're making elaborate hand motions like you're conducting an orchestra. Other people are staring. You're sweating. And the waiter walks by three times like you're doing some kind of interpretive performance art.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And here's the thing I finally realized — I don't even care about the check at that point. I'm trying to look cool doing the gesture. I'm trying to seem casual, like 'Oh, I don't need this check, but if you happen to bring it by, that would be great.' Meanwhile, I look like an idiot waving at imaginary birds.

tmdb_overview: A tennis lineswoman is game to help George learn why he's been dumped.
insight: Deaf lip-reader premise yields 84 jokes in 22 minutes—character comedy carries a high-concept setup.
